poem:
**Waxing | Waning**

Glasgow, where rain keeps time.  
Three lads in a room: "not jazz", sludge, a soldering-iron hiss.  
Sax squawks like a gull in a bin,  
guitar bites—industrial, blunt-toothed—  
drums tiptoe then kick the door in.  
They play chicken with silence,  
and laugh when it swerves.
prompt: You are the poet Simon Armitage. You woke up this morning feeling creative. You have just read this story in the newspaper: "Add to playlist: the disaster-baiting jazz-rock brinkmanship of Taupe and the week’s best new tracks From Glasgow, ScotlandRecommended if you like Horse Lords, Melt-Banana, abrasive saxophoneUp next Album out now, touring the UK and Ireland from June Taupe’s lawless mix of “not jazz”, sludgy rock and homemade electronics hits like a shock of cold water to the face. The Glasgow-based trio are a formidable live band: thunderously loud, crushingly tight, quick to surrender all control and trust-fall their way through wild improvisations. Their third album, Waxing | Waning, out now on Prague’s Minority Records, finally captures that power, as well as the band’s oddball humour and free-flowing imagination. Single Lemonade Tycoon (a nod to the early-2000s lemonade stall video game) opens with stilted, comedic flurries of squawking sax and tip-toeing percussion before finding its groove – as well as ragged electric guitar with the heft of an industrial power tool. Sharp, smart and often brutal, Taupe make light work of heavy noise, as well as the complex musicianship that underpins their sound. Guitarist Mike Parr-Burman and saxophonist Jamie Stockbridge met studying music at Newcastle University and, like drummer Alex Palmer, are tied to a clutch of other experiments and projects, including the Glasgow Improvisers Orchestra. But, as Taupe, the trio share a brinkmanship – a mutual understanding that risk of disaster is part of the fun. Allcapsallbold melts a repeated melody into purring electronic chaos and then again into stomping doom metal, while Pet Boss evolves from a freaky sound". Write a poem in YOUR OWN DISTINCTIVE STYLE, no more than 60 words long. You may add a one line title at the top if you like.
news: Add to playlist: the disaster-baiting jazz-rock brinkmanship of Taupe and the week’s best new tracks From Glasgow, ScotlandRecommended if you like Horse Lords, Melt-Banana, abrasive saxophoneUp next Album out now, touring the UK and Ireland from June Taupe’s lawless mix of “not jazz”, sludgy rock and homemade electronics hits like a shock of cold water to the face. The Glasgow-based trio are a formidable live band: thunderously loud, crushingly tight, quick to surrender all control and trust-fall their way through wild improvisations. Their third album, Waxing | Waning, out now on Prague’s Minority Records, finally captures that power, as well as the band’s oddball humour and free-flowing imagination. Single Lemonade Tycoon (a nod to the early-2000s lemonade stall video game) opens with stilted, comedic flurries of squawking sax and tip-toeing percussion before finding its groove – as well as ragged electric guitar with the heft of an industrial power tool. Sharp, smart and often brutal, Taupe make light work of heavy noise, as well as the complex musicianship that underpins their sound. Guitarist Mike Parr-Burman and saxophonist Jamie Stockbridge met studying music at Newcastle University and, like drummer Alex Palmer, are tied to a clutch of other experiments and projects, including the Glasgow Improvisers Orchestra. But, as Taupe, the trio share a brinkmanship – a mutual understanding that risk of disaster is part of the fun. Allcapsallbold melts a repeated melody into purring electronic chaos and then again into stomping doom metal, while Pet Boss evolves from a freaky sound
